turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: In case number 16-349, Henson versus Santander Consumer USA Inc., Justice Gorsuch has his first opinion for the court.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Thank you, Chief. The Fair Debt Collection Practices Act authorizes private lawsuits and weighty fines to deter wayward debt collection practices. But of course, the act's rigors apply only to persons who actually qualify as debt collectors, a term of art the statute defines to include those who “Regularly collect debts owed or due another.” Now, everyone agrees this definition embraces the repo man, someone hired to collect outstanding debts of another person. Everyone also agrees the definition excludes the originator of a loan who seeks to collect that debt for its own account, not for the account of another person. But what if you purchase a debt admittedly originated by another person and then try to collect it for yourself for your own account? Does that make you a debt collector subject to federal law? That is the nub of the dispute we face today. We agree with the judgment of the Fourth Circuit under review and hold that a debt purchaser may collect debts for its own account without triggering the statutory definition and dispute. By defining debt collectors to include those who regularly collect debts owed another, the statute's plain language focuses on third party collection agents, who are in the business of collecting debts owed to someone else. It does not capture those like ordinary loan originators and debt purchasers who simply seek to collect what is owed to them alone. The petitioner's arguments to the contrary do not dislodge the Statute's plain terms. Petitioners point out that the word “owed” is a past participle of the verb, “to owe” and so suggests that the debt collector definition must embrace debt purchasers because they necessarily collect debts that are formally owed in the past to someone else. The trouble is, in ordinary English, past participles like “owed” are regularly used as adjectives to describe the present state of a thing. So for example you might say, “The burnt toast is not edible; a fallen branch blocks the path.” And equally here, you might say that a debt owner wishes to collect a debt currently owed to you. Notably, Congress used the word “owed” in just this way as referring to present, and not past, debt relationships in a variety of places throughout the act. And petitioners have not rebutted the presumption that the same words and the same statute should carry the same meaning. Alternatively, petitioners contend that their interpretation would better further the act's perceived purposes. They contend that at the time that the Congress passed the act, it did not have much experience with debt purchasers. And they say subsequent experience teaches that debt purchasers should be treated like third-party debt collectors and brought within the act's gambit as a matter of good policy. The problem is that it is not this Court's proper role to rewrite a constitutionally valid statue under the banner of speculation about what Congress might have done had it faced a question that by everyone's admission it never faced. Reasonable people can disagree over how well the act balances the various social costs and benefits in this area, and some might wish to revisit that balance. But policy questions like these are matters for Congress, and not this Court. In our legal order, it is the job of the Judiciary to apply, not amend, the work of the people's representatives. The judgment is affirmed.
